meta_title: For Students: email automation to Solve no project ideas (Intermediate Tutorial)
meta_description: Learn how to email automation and overcome no project ideas. A Intermediate-level Tutorial using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: Email Automation to Solve No Project Ideas
content.seo_description: Stop struggling with no project ideas. Build a powerful email automation tool using Antigravity and Claude Code. Perfect for intermediate student portfolios.
content.answer_block: Break the cycle of 'tutorial hell' by building a real-world email automation engine. By integrating Antigravity for rapid backend scaffolding and Claude Code for intelligent implementation, you'll create a functional tool that manages newsletter workflows or lead outreach, instantly adding a high-impact, production-ready asset to your developer portfolio.
content.intro:
The screen stares back, a blinking cursor mocking your lack of inspiration. You've watched the tutorials, you know the syntax, but your GitHub remains a graveyard of half-finished 'To-Do' apps. The problem isn't your coding ability; it's the lack of a tangible, high-value project that proves you can solve actual business problems.

Today, we shift from student to builder. We are constructing an automated email pipeline—a project that mimics the architecture of high-growth SaaS companies. By leveraging Antigravity and Claude Code, you will stop fighting the boilerplate and start shipping logic that impacts real users. It is time to build something that actually matters.

steps.title: Define the Automation Logic
steps.description: Before writing code, define the trigger, the data source, and the email template. This ensures your architecture is modular.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Scaffold a Python-based backend service architecture for an email automation tool. Include modules for trigger handling, template rendering using Jinja2, and a mock provider interface for email delivery.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'create project structure with /src, /tests, and /config folders'

steps.title: Configure the Email Provider Interface
steps.description: Create an abstraction layer for your email provider (like SendGrid or Resend) to ensure you can switch services without breaking your app.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a clean interface for an EmailProvider class with methods for send_email and validate_address. Include error handling for SMTP failures.
steps.claude_code_command: claude edit 'implement email_provider.py with an abstraction layer for SMTP and API-based delivery'

steps.title: Implement the Template Engine
steps.description: Use Jinja2 to dynamically inject user data into your email templates, transforming static messages into personalized communications.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a template service that loads HTML files, injects dictionary-based variables, and returns a sanitized string for the email body.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'create templates/welcome.html and implement logic to render it with dynamic user data'

steps.title: Build the Queue Worker
steps.description: Don't send emails synchronously. Use a basic queue system to ensure your app remains responsive during high-volume operations.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Design a simple queue worker pattern using Python's asyncio to process email tasks from a local list or database table.
steps.claude_code_command: claude edit 'add an asynchronous queue processor to main.py to handle email dispatching in the background'

steps.title: Testing and Deployment Readiness
steps.description: Validate your flow with unit tests to ensure that every trigger correctly results in a dispatched email.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a suite of pytest unit tests that mock the EmailProvider and verify that the template service renders correctly.

pro_tips: Always use Environment Variables for API keys; never hardcode credentials, even in local development.
Implement 'Dry Run' mode in your code that logs emails to the console instead of sending them, saving you from accidental spamming during testing.
Use a dedicated service like Mailtrap for local development to catch outgoing emails without sending them to real recipients.
Keep your email templates separate from your logic; it makes updating content significantly easier for non-technical team members.
Add logging to every step of the email pipeline; debugging silent failures in automated systems is the hardest part of building them.
content.next_steps: Once your local automation is working, deploy your code to a free tier on Render or Fly.io, and try adding a simple database to track email open rates.
content.conversion_block: You now have a production-grade project on your machine. Don't stop here—iterate on it, add a dashboard, and show the world what you can build. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
